---
title: "Chapman's Blazing Star (Liatris chapmanii)"
slug: "liatris-chapmanii"
group: "Plantae"
image: "https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/577048001/medium.jpg"        # keep for thumbnails
image-alt: "Chapman's Blazing Star"  # keep for a11y
tax_kingdom: "Plantae"
tax_class: "Magnoliopsida"
tax_order: "Asterales"
tax_family: "Asteraceae"
categories: ["Plantae", "Magnoliopsida", "Asterales", "Asteraceae"]   # these drive the chips + filtering
freeze: true
---

<figure class="species-figure">

<img src="https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/577048001/medium.jpg" alt="Chapman's Blazing Star" class="species-photo"/>

<figcaption class="photo-credit">

Photo: <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/people/dsedore">
dsedore</a> (CC BY-NC) via <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/319237445">iNaturalist</a>

</figcaption>

</figure>

## Taxonomy

-   **Class:** Magnoliopsida
-   **Order:** Asterales
-   **Family:** Asteraceae

## Occurrence at DeLuca Preserve

-   [DeLuca Bio Blitz Species Observations](https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?project_id=191727&subview=map&taxon_id=164683)

## Conservation & rarity context

-   IUCN: Not Listed
-   **Rarity note:** Uncommon (scattered) species in Florida found in dry, sandy habitats such as scrub and sandhills.

## Natural history notes

A perennial wildflower characterized by its narrow leaves and spikes of bright purple flower heads arranged along upright stems. In Florida, it is found in dry, sandy habitats such as scrub and sandhills, particularly in central parts of the state. It typically blooms in late summer to fall and attracts a variety of pollinators, including butterflies and bees.

## References / links

-   [iNaturalist species page](https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/164683-Liatris-chapmanii)
-   [Local profile](https://www.fnps.org/plant/liatris-chapmanii#gsc.tab=0)
